narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 78% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows solid choices like planning to attend full-time, but it highlights some challenges: your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 is notably below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 22 falls well short of the usual 36.70, and your school deciles—3rd in Year 1 and 1st in Year 3—are lower than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, respectively. Coming from a middle-income family puts you right around the norm, while being a female Asian student aligns with smaller but present groups in the data (about 44% female and 6% Asian overall). The model weighed these factors to predict higher risk of bar failure, with your Year 3 decile having the strongest pull toward that outcome, followed closely by your LSAT score. Your Year 1 decile came next in influence, also nudging the prediction toward failure, then your family income bracket, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender in descending order of impact. Each of these pushed the model's thinking in the direction of bar exam struggles rather than success, with no single factor overriding the rest but their combined low performance compared to typical admitted students tipping the scale. In essence, students who succeed on the bar often have stronger high school deciles, higher LSATs, and better GPAs, and your metrics fell short in those key areas relative to the dataset. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 36, strengthen your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to boost your effective GPA, and build study habits that could have lifted your school deciles. Demographics like gender, race, and family income aren't changeable, but excelling in academics often outweighs them in these predictions. Consider targeted bar prep resources early, as they help many students defy initial odds. With dedication to these steps, you could reapply stronger next cycle and shift the model's view in your favor. Hang in there—many have turned similar profiles around.
